# Monday's Early Bird: Quietly breaking another promise

### Winston says Govt to end fees-free in Budget; Luxon says National would raise retirement age immediately; 144 Police officers leave for Australia in last year; Renters give up on home-ownership

[![Bernard Hickey's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TDHa!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F85f4e334-2972-4620-9c59-202ce3e81cde_800x500.jpeg)](https://substack.com/@bernardchickey)

[Bernard Hickey](https://substack.com/@bernardchickey)

May 10, 2026

∙ Paid

30

5

5

Share

Briefly this morning before my Chorus Substack Live video briefing for paying subscribers [here at 9 am](https://open.substack.com/live-stream/196761?utm_source=live-stream-scheduled-upsell), here’s the key news in Aotearoa’s political economy over the last day or two around housing, climate and poverty:

1. The Government will dump the final first year of fees-free tertiary education in the Budget later this month, adding to its ending of first home buyer subsidy grants as unpromised budget cuts hitting young voters hardest [1](https://thekaka.substack.com/p/mondays-early-bird-quietly-breaking#footnote-1).

2. The world’s oil stocks are being drained at an unprecedented pace, worrying oil industry experts that another sharp spike in oil prices towards US$200/barrel could happen within weeks [2](https://thekaka.substack.com/p/mondays-early-bird-quietly-breaking#footnote-2).

3. **Police Commissioner Richard Chambers** has conceded to _1News_ he can’t compete with the salaries and incentives being offered by Australian police forces, with at least 144 officers leaving for Australia in the past year [3](https://thekaka.substack.com/p/mondays-early-bird-quietly-breaking#footnote-3).

4. A ‘poll of polls’ analysing the trend of support for the coalition of parties in Government parties vs the Opposition parties shows the Government is on track to win re-election, thanks to a surge in support for **NZ First** [4](https://thekaka.substack.com/p/mondays-early-bird-quietly-breaking#footnote-4).

5. A survey [5](https://thekaka.substack.com/p/mondays-early-bird-quietly-breaking#footnote-5) of 506 young New Zealanders for **OneChoice** has found 54% now define the ‘New Zealand dream’ as being financially independent, ahead of home-ownership (44%), while 65% say hoping to own a home is no longer relevant.

6. The survey found 33% of renters spend at least half their income on rent, with a further 38% spending between 30% and 49% of their income on rent, while 71% are delaying starting a family and/or changing careers due to housing pressures, and 76% feel ‘trapped’ as renters being unable to save for a deposit.


_Paying subscribers get the full Picks n’ Mixes below and access to Substack Live sessions. A full recorded video and podcast of today’s Chorus goes out to paying subscribers later today._

By [Angus Whitley](https://www.bloomberg.com/authors/AEaJLmK35vQ/angus-whitley) and [Toby Alder](https://www.bloomberg.com/authors/ADtpfRQk0Nc/toby-alder)

May 10, 2026 at 6:48 PM EDT

BookmarkSave

Translate

Happy Monday morning. Any hope of a steadier US-Iran ceasefire has taken another hit, with Donald Trump [knocking back](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/trump-rejects-latest-iran-peace-offer-as-totally-unacceptable) Tehran’s latest peace overture. Closer to home, healthcare giant CSL cut its full-year outlook, while One Nation is riding a fresh [wave of momentum](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/far-right-australian-party-wins-seat-in-first-election-victory) after its weekend by-election win. And in Canberra, Treasurer Jim Chalmers is setting the tone ahead of tomorrow’s budget, telling Bloomberg the current settings on housing and tax are “unfair and unacceptable” _—_ [**_Angus Whitley_**](https://www.bloomberg.com/authors/AEaJLmK35vQ/angus-whitley "Angus Whitley  | Bloomberg") _,_ **_Global Business reporter_**

### What’s happening now

**Australian biotech CSL Ltd.** cut its [full-year outlook](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/csl-cuts-profit-outlook-flags-5-billion-in-new-impairments) and flagged about $5 billion in additional impairments, saying a turnaround will take longer than expected after a review by its interim chief executive. The firm now expects revenue of about $15.2 billion and net profit of about $3.1 billion for the year ending June 30, 2026.

**Far-right Australian party** One Nation [won](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/far-right-australian-party-wins-seat-in-first-election-victory "Far-Right Australian Party Wins Seat in First Election Victory") its first lower house parliamentary seat with David Farley securing almost 60% of the vote in the seat of Farrer in Saturday’s by-election. In his victory speech, he criticized immigrants who “give us nothing” and vowed to target “damn net-zero programs.”

**Australia’s upcoming budget** will [take aim](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-09/australia-to-tackle-unacceptable-housing-market-chalmers-says) at soaring home prices and seek to make it easier for people to gain a foothold in the housing market, Treasurer Jim Chalmers said in an [interview](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/videos/2026-05-09/chalmers-on-unacceptable-australian-housing-status-quo-video). The government is mulling changes to tax concessions — including negative gearing and capital gains tax — which have been widely viewed as favoring property investors, according to recent reports.

Expand

![Jim Chalmers at Parliament House in Canberra on May 10. Photographer: Hilary Wardhaugh/Bloomberg](https://assets.bwbx.io/images/users/iqjWHBFdfxIU/ivhA97IWdC.U/v0/-1x-1.webp)
Jim Chalmers at Parliament House in Canberra on May 10.Photographer: Hilary Wardhaugh/Bloomberg

**Worries in the private credit market** stem from retail-driven liquidity problems rather than the health of the underlining portfolio, Frank Kwok, chief financial officer at Macquarie Group Ltd., [said in an interview](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-08/macquarie-cfo-says-private-credit-angst-due-to-liquidity-issues). Concerns have bubbled in private credit markets this year, with investors spooked by the risk of technological disruptions and access to liquidity.

Expand

![The Macquarie Group Ltd. logo at the company's headquarters in Sydney, Australia, on Wednesday, June 18, 2025. Macquarie Group has completed an A$686 million share purchase to help fund bonus and profit share payments to employees. Photographer: Brent Lewin/Bloomberg](https://assets.bwbx.io/images/users/iqjWHBFdfxIU/iHygn8ijSzyQ/v7/-1x-1.webp)
The Macquarie Group Ltd. logo at the company’s headquarters in Sydney.Photographer: Brent Lewin/Bloomberg

**Australia’s military** is ready to [deploy with its allies](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-08/australia-sees-military-allies-ready-to-work-together-if-needed) if needed in Asia or beyond, its defense forces chief said at the conclusion of military drills in the South China Sea with the US, Philippines and others. Australia aims to conduct more military activities, including training with countries like Japan and Indonesia, Admiral David Johnston said in an interview in Manila.

[![](https://assets.bwbx.io/images/users/iqjWHBFdfxIU/ieTTU0YjK55I/v0/-1x-1.webp)](https://www.bloomberg.com/podcasts/series/bloomberg-australia)

**The Australian government** is moving to tighten an [annual performance test](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-08/australia-moves-to-toughen-pension-funds-performance-scorecard) for the nation’s A$4.5 trillion ($3.2 trillion) pension industry as concerns grow the current model may be steering investment away from some sectors. The government has launched a public consultation on the test, which measures pension fund performance against a set of benchmarks.

### What happened overnight

**President Donald Trump** labeled Iran’s latest response to his proposal to end the 10-week conflict with the US as “ [TOTALLY UNACCEPTABLE](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/trump-rejects-latest-iran-peace-offer-as-totally-unacceptable)” as the two sides struggle to maintain a fragile ceasefire. Iran offered to transfer some of its stockpile of highly enriched uranium to a third country, but rejected the idea of dismantling its nuclear facilities, the Wall Street Journal reported earlier.

Meanwhile, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said in an [interview with CBS’ _60 Minutes_](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/netanyahu-says-war-with-iran-isn-t-over-need-to-secure-uranium) that the war with Iran is “not over” because there’s nuclear material in the country that still needs to be removed.

**Investors riding** a scorching run of market momentum are likely to face a [reality check](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/momentum-obsessed-traders-seek-clues-on-iran-truce-markets-wrap) when trading resumes Sunday night New York time after President Trump rejected the latest peace offering. The dollar was higher against major peers in early trading.

Expand

![Wall Street signage during the Hawkeye 360 Inc. initial public offering (IPO) at the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) in New York, US, on Thursday, May 7, 2026. Hawkeye 360 Inc., a provider of satellite-based signals intelligence for US government agencies, is raising $416 million in a US initial public offering priced at the top of a marketed range, according to people familiar with the matter. Photographer: Michael Nagle/Bloomberg](https://assets.bwbx.io/images/users/iqjWHBFdfxIU/iypGCoTS9zrg/v8/-1x-1.webp)
Photographer: Michael Nagle/Bloomberg

**President Trump** is expected to press President Xi Jinping over China’s approach to Iran and hammer out details on a new board of trade when they meet this week in Beijing, [senior US officials said](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/trump-aims-to-press-xi-over-china-s-approach-to-conflict-in-iran). Trump and Xi are scheduled for talks on Thursday and Friday as they wrestle with strong disagreements over trade and the US-Israeli war with Iran.

**UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer** will make a last-ditch effort to [save his premiership](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-10/starmer-s-uk-rivals-circle-ahead-of-make-or-break-reset-speech) on Monday with a politically charged address designed to hold back calls for a change in leadership atop Britain’s governing Labour Party. The speech will follow Labour’s sweeping defeat in local elections on Thursday, including a historic loss of control in the Welsh parliament.

### What to watch

• No major events scheduled

### One more thing...

**Once ridiculed**, Reform UK’s Nigel Farage is closing in on his quest to rule the UK after a host of council areas switched to his party in [local elections](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-08/how-bad-for-labour-britain-s-local-elections-in-six-charts).

# The richer Australians get, the sadder they become, data shows

By [Alan Austin](https://independentaustralia.net/profile-on/alan-austin,67 "View all of this authors articles") \| 11 May 2026, 8:00am \| [6](https://independentaustralia.net/politics/politics-display/the-richer-australians-get-the-sadder-they-become-data-shows,21024#disqus_thread) comments \| [PDF Printer Friendly](https://independentaustralia.net/pdf/ia-print.php?article=21024 "PDF Printer Friendly")

![](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w800-c650x433/i/article/img/article-21024-hero.jpg?t=1778404356)Australia in 2026: financially comfortable, spiritually buffering (Image by Dan Jensen)

_While the economy recorded its best year ever in 2025, life dissatisfaction hit a new low, as [Alan Austin](https://independentaustralia.net/profile-on/alan-austin,67) reports._

PERHAPS WE NEED a name for this syndrome. It is an intriguing phenomenon, but also deeply troubling, as it reveals declining mental health.

As Australians achieved their highest material living standards ever last year, their enjoyment of life and community engagement, as assessed by the Bureau of Statistics ( [ABS](https://www.abs.gov.au/)), plummeted.

As [discussed](https://independentaustralia.net/business/business-display/australians-are-miserable-and-anxious-because-the-media-coaches-them-,19529) early last year, Australians have the best life of anyone on the planet, based on objective criteria, but ranked a miserable tenth on Oxford’s [Happiness Index](https://www.worldhappiness.report/). In this year’s rankings, Australians [tumbled](https://independentaustralia.net/business/business-display/murdoch-led-mainstreammedia-makes-australians-sad,20843) to 15th.

[![Class is the missing link in Australia’s generational inequality crisis](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w580-c650x433/i/article/img/article-21019-thumb.jpg)](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/class-is-the-missing-link-in-australias-generational-inequality-crisis,21019)

##### [Class is the missing link in Australia’s generational inequality crisis](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/class-is-the-missing-link-in-australias-generational-inequality-crisis,21019)

[Australia’s housing crisis isn’t just a clash between generations, but a deepening class divide hidden beneath the rhetoric of the “fair crack”.](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/class-is-the-missing-link-in-australias-generational-inequality-crisis,21019)

#### **Highest living standards ever**

We now know 2025 was the [first time](https://independentaustralia.net/politics/politics-display/specialists-refuse-to-correctly-diagnose-best-economy-ever,20897) on record that Australians achieved these four excellent outcomes simultaneously over a full year:

- jobless below 4.5%;
- inflation below 4%;
- interest rates between 3% to 5%; and
- annual GDP growth above 2.5%.

Australia was the only country in 2025 to enjoy those four outcomes plus triple A ratings with all major credit agencies. Denmark, Norway, Saudi Arabia, Israel and Vietnam ticked four of those five boxes, but only Australia ticked all five — in the world!

Datasets confirming the best-ever quality of life include:

- record number of overseas [flights](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/industry/tourism-and-transport/overseas-arrivals-and-departures-australia/latest-release): 12.55 million;
- [median wealth](https://www.ubs.com/global/en/wealthmanagement/insights/global-wealth-report.html) second highest in the world: US$268,424 (AU$370,137);
- record percentage of students attending fee-paying [private schools](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/people/education/schools/latest-release): 37.2%;
- record [home loans](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/economy/finance/lending-indicators/latest-release): $383.5 billion;
- record consumer [spending](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/economy/national-accounts/australian-national-accounts-finance-and-wealth/latest-release) to GDP;
- record percentage of retail spending on [dining out](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/economy/finance/monthly-household-spending-indicator/latest-release): 13.8%;
- record consumer spending on cosmetics, jewellery and other [luxuries](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/economy/finance/monthly-household-spending-indicator/latest-release);
- record sales of [electric cars](https://www.drive.com.au/news/australian-new-car-sales-in-april-2026-ev-sales-share-breaks-another-vfacts-record/);
- record single and twin-engined [light aircraft](https://www.casa.gov.au/aircraft/aircraft-registration/data-files-registered-aircraft#Tipstodownloadthefiles) registered;
- record high [jobs](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/labour/employment-and-unemployment/labour-force-australia/latest-release) to population: 64.6%;
- record [minimum wage](https://tradingeconomics.com/australia/minimum-wages), [pensions](https://guides.dss.gov.au/social-security-guide/5/2/2/10) and benefits, all up more than inflation; and
- record families in [social housing](https://www.pc.gov.au/ongoing/report-on-government-services/2026/data-downloads/): 429,212.

[![Global agencies give Australia’s economy thumbs up](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w580-c650x433/i/article/img/article-20972-thumb.jpg)](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/global-agencies-give-australias-economy-thumbs-up,20972)

##### [Global agencies give Australia’s economy thumbs up](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/global-agencies-give-australias-economy-thumbs-up,20972)

[As job losses, inflation and weak growth impact many economies, Australia advances apace.](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/global-agencies-give-australias-economy-thumbs-up,20972)

#### **Life satisfaction in severe decline**

According to last Tuesday’s ABS general social survey results, life satisfaction in 2025 was well down on 2019, when Australia’s economy was close to the worst-performed among OECD members.

Overall life satisfaction in 2019 was rated high or very high by 77.6% of the population. That plummeted to 68.3% in 2025. Only 6.2% of citizens experienced low life satisfaction in 2019. That jumped to 9.7% in 2025.

Yet, as the International Monetary Fund ( [IMF](http://www.imf.org/)) [reported](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/weo/2026/april/english/text.pdf) last month, on overall budget balance, Australia ranked last among developed nations in 2019. The economy trailed badly then also on economic growth, government debt, global [innovation](https://www.wipo.int/en/web/global-innovation-index) and the jobless rate.

#### **Mainstream newsrooms continue to foment fear and anxiety**

The problem, of course, is that most Aussies have no idea how well they are travelling relative to past periods and to the rest of the world.

The news media steadfastly refused to report last year’s impressive results, claiming instead that the Australian dream was _‘ [dead](https://thewest.com.au/opinion/the-australian-dream-is-dead-and-our-leaders-are-dancing-on-its-grave-in-high-viz-vests-c-21513706)’_, monetary policy was a _‘ [horror scenario](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-02-03/rba-decision-to-raise-interest-rates-horror-scenario-outlined/106300520)’_ and a _‘ [failed experiment](https://www.news.com.au/finance/economy/interest-rates/reserve-banks-failed-experiment-haunts-australia-as-interest-rates-rise-again/news-story/4ced52b980d700e52c1979949c926f44#:~:text=Reserve%20Bank's%20'failed%20experiment'%20haunts,us%20that%20this%20wa)’_ haunting Australia, whose very _‘ [survival](https://www.theaustralian.com.au/breaking-news/survival-gross-work-trend-impacting-australian-workforce/news-story/05500211f7c409af0b3516f2910f5346)’_ is at stake, and the economy was a _‘ [nightmare](https://www.theage.com.au/politics/federal/inflation-results-are-a-nightmare-for-jim-chalmers-and-michele-bullock-20251029-p5n63d.html)’_, a _‘ [crisis](https://www.9news.com.au/national/cost-of-living-crisis-australia-families-struggling-instagram-video/cd6f6f2c-2b9f-4750-b83f-bb0fc4b65a83)’_ and a _‘ [dire reality](https://newsapp.abc.net.au/news/2025-10-16/essential-workers-priced-out-of-rental-housing-queensland/105893520)’_.

Last week’s jump in the interest rate provided another example.

Interest rates are finally [climbing](https://www.rba.gov.au/publications/smp/2026/may/pdf/statement-on-monetary-policy-2026-05.pdf) towards 5%, offering relief to long-suffering savers who rely on interest to survive, but have been denied a fair go since 2013.

Interest rates are now smack in the middle of the optimum range, 3% to 5%, which balances the needs of both savers and home buyers. See chart below.

[![](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w1000-c2344x1228/https://independentaustralia.net/sc/Interest%20rates%201991%20to%20May%2026%20IA.jpg)](https://independentaustralia.net/#)(Data source: [Reserve Bank](https://www.rba.gov.au/))

#### **Newsrooms distort the reality**

The RBA’s [decision](https://www.rba.gov.au/speeches/2026/mc-gov-2026-05-05.html) should have been welcomed as an inspired outcome. It dampens the risk of inflation accelerating while disadvantaging neither borrowers nor lenders. Win-win-win.

Instead, it was depicted as _‘ [brutal](https://www.news.com.au/finance/economy/australian-economy/expert-claims-rba-has-guaranteed-lower-living-standards-after-brutal-interest-rate-hike/news-story/542b3830fc100e5e609db8e66993b0dc)’_, _‘ [disastrous](https://www.9news.com.au/national/how-much-will-the-rba-interest-rate-hike-cost-you/6568da42-5c43-4c97-a70d-79d03330cfc5)’_, _‘ [risking recession](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ozmQzgp-6Uc)’_, _‘ [another blow](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OeWAijKYRDI)’_ and _‘ [pouring petrol on the inflation fire](https://www.smh.com.au/politics/federal/reserve-bank-raises-interest-rates-for-third-time-this-year-20260505-p5ztup.html)’_.

The Murdoch tabloids [said](https://www.cairnspost.com.au/subscribe/news/1/?sourceCode=CPWEB_WRE170_a&dest=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cairnspost.com.au%2Fbusiness%2Fopinion-analysis%2Freserve-bank-pushes-australia-into-1990sstyle-recession-after-shock-demand-collapse%2Fnews-story%2Fd09ef87b34): _‘We’re inching closer to the recession we didn’t need to have’_. ABC News [falsified](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/programs/am/opposition-blames-labor-for-inflation-rate-hike/106646354) the record with: _‘Opposition blames Labor for inflation, rate hike’_. Sky News, fuelling Australia’s deteriorating mental health, [declared](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N5HW_kSj2t4): _‘People are giving up hope’_. Absolute destructive nonsense!

Yes, mortgagees will now pay more. But they can [negotiate](https://www.mortgageadvicebureau.com.au/refinancing/) the repayment schedule to avoid hardship and will eventually [recover](https://www.banqueducanada.ca/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/swp2026-1.pdf?utm_source=copilot.com) more than the extra outlays via the associated increase in property values.

#### **Abundant evidence that all is well**

The RBA’s [report](https://www.rba.gov.au/speeches/2026/mc-gov-2026-05-05.html) last Tuesday contained remarkably upbeat forecasts, given the global turmoil resulting from the madness of U.S. President [Trump](https://independentaustralia.net/politics/politics-display/donald-trump-is-trashing-the-australian-economy,21001)’s kleptocracy.

GDP growth is expected to be 1.9% this financial year and 1.3% in the next. Yes, that’s historically weak, but nowhere near a recession.

Inflation is forecast at 4.8% for June this year and just 2.4% at June next year. Given inflation [varied](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/research/70-years-inflation-australia) between 7.5% and 18% for most of the period from June 1973 to June 1990, that is extremely benign.

Surprisingly, the jobless rate, which is now 4.26%, is expected to be 4.2% this June and 4.4% in June 2027.

All these compare favourably with the rest of the world.

In fact, Australia’s [inflation](https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/economy/price-indexes-and-inflation/consumer-price-index-australia/latest-release) rate since January has risen just 0.73%. The [increments](https://tradingeconomics.com/country-list/inflation-rate) have been above 1% in Austria, Finland, Portugal, Poland, Turkey and Chile; higher than 1.8% in France, Italy, Luxembourg and the USA; and above 2.8% in Belgium and Greece.

##### [Why citizens in such a wealthy country are angry and depressed](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/why-citizens-in-such-a-wealthy-country-are-angry-and-depressed,20747)

[The more their quality of life improves, the sadder some Australians seem to become.](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/why-citizens-in-such-a-wealthy-country-are-angry-and-depressed,20747)

#### **Supportive behaviour impacted**

Last week’s ABS social survey shows that Australians' engagement in the community has declined sharply as wealth has increased and misery has advanced.

Citizens who undertook unpaid voluntary work fell from 29.5% in 2019 to 22.6% in 2025. Those who provided unpaid support for non-household members dropped from 51.5% to 45.4%. Participants in community support groups slipped from 25.3% to just 20.7%.

Disturbingly, face-to-face contact with family or friends living outside the household tumbled from 67.8% to just 52.9%.

So Australians are not just more despondent but also less practically helpful.

Newsrooms telling the truth would fix this. Now, there’s a happy thought.

D

This is a case where psychology takes precedence over economics. There is no room for all the evidence but the first paradox was reported after WWII when people reported that that was the happiest period in their lives when London was being blitzed and the situation was grim. It was because they had never felt so close, such a sense of belonging. The give aways in Alan's report are the dwindling figures for volunteering, unpaid support, participation in community and face to face contact with family and friends. This marks a trend toward what is technically known as dissociation, essentially the privatization of our lives, producing isolated, lonely people who as a result of their isolation can become distrustful and ultimately aggressive. The trend began in the late 1970s and has waxed and waned. It has likely become stronger as a result of the last few years of Coalition rule but hopefully should start declining from now on if we can revive our communities.

# Australia's top banker Michele Bullock issues brutal message to Anthony Albanese's government

Story by Caitlin Powell

Key takeaways

![InfoIcon](https://assets.msn.com/staticsb/statics/latest/icons-wc/icons/InfoIconLight.svg)

Curated by Copilot

Copilot uses AI to shape what you see here. Mistakes happen, so double-check the facts.

- Interest Rate Hike: The Reserve Bank of Australia raised the cash rate by 0.25 points to 4.35%, aiming to slow demand and curb persistent inflation, which is now 4.6% annually.
- Government Spending Impact: Governor Michele Bullock criticized the Albanese government, saying its high spending and household handouts worsen inflation by increasing demand beyond supply.
- Mortgage Pressure: For a $600,000 mortgage, the latest hike adds $91/month, with total increases since February reaching $272/month, pushing average variable rates above 6.25%.

The Reserve Bank governor has unleashed at the Albanese government, saying its spending while resources are limited is a major factor in persistent inflation.

At its meeting on Tuesday, the RBA board headed by Michelle Bullock decided to increase the cash rate by 0.25 points to 4.35 per cent in a troubling blow for Australian mortgage holders.

The decision was prompted by persistent inflation, which Treasurer [Jim Chalmers](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/jim-chalmers/index.html) earlier this year claimed the government had tamed. Australian Bureau of Statistics revealed last week that headline [inflation](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/inflation/index.html) had picked up to 1.1 per cent in March.

Annual inflation is at 4.6 per cent, up from 3.7 per cent, which marks the fastest annual pace of price growth since September 2023.

In a statement, the RBA noted that inflation had picked up 'materially' in the second half of 2025, and had then been boosted because of the conflict in the Middle East.

A regular refrain from Ms Bullock in her 45-minute press conference on Tuesday was that the board was working to stop inflation, which is already 'too high'. The only tool the RBA has to achieve this is to raise cash rate, which dampens spending.

But Bullock brutally took aim at the Albanese government's attempts to buy votes.

'The extent to which (the) government makes up (budget) shortfalls for households by giving them more money makes it harder to dampen demand,' she said.

She said even before the Iran conflict broke out, demand in Australia was above supply, which raises prices.

'The ability of the economy to supply the goods and services that were being demanded in total, including by government and by the private sector, was outstripping the ability of the economy to supply it.

'That's why inflation was rising.'

Ms Bullock highlighted that the RBA decision was a move to slow down demand.

'If we are increasing interest rates, what we are trying to do is slow growth in demand and that hits the private sector – typically consumption, investment, those sorts of things,' she said.

'The extent that the government is demanding goods and services of the economy - whether it's direct expenditure or giving money to households to spend on goods and services in the private sector - that adds to demand.'

Ms Bullock went on to say that that Australians have been left poorer in the wake of surging oil prices amid the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

She also warned more interest rate increases could be on the way as inflation threatens to climb further if the government does not tighten its spending.

'Developments in the Middle East remain highly uncertain but under a wide range of possible scenarios, the conflict adds to global and domestic inflation,' she said.

'If left unchecked, higher costs get embedded into price and wage-setting decisions.

'These second-round effects could lead to even higher and more persistent inflation and if so, would require even more tightening in monetary policy to get inflation under control.'

For an owner‑occupier with a $600,000 mortgage and 25 years remaining at the start of this year's hikes, another 0.25 adds $91 to their minimum monthly repayments.

The total increase across the three hikes since February would be $272 a month.

The increase will lift the average owner‑occupier variable rate to 6.26 per cent, pushing it above the 6.25 per cent mark for the first time since January 2025.

Earlier on Tuesday, Treasurer Jim Chalmers had attempted to blame the interest rate hike solely on the war the United States and Israel undertook against Iran.

'Australians are already paying a hefty price for the war in the Middle East and this decision will make it tougher,' Chalmers said in a statement.

'What we saw in the month of March was overwhelmingly a story of higher petrol prices before our fuel tax cut kicked in. Higher petrol prices are all about the war in the Middle East,' he said.

'Any objective observer would conclude that now. When it comes to decisions taken in Washington DC, or indeed in Tehran, Australians are hostage to those decisions taken about the conduct of this war and the end of this war.'

The decision to hike interest rates is just one week before the Treasurer is due to announce the Labor government's fifth Federal Budget.

'The uncertainty and volatility in the global economy mean there is an even greater premium on responsible fiscal management,' he said on Tuesday.

'The budget is already more than $233billion better than we inherited because the Government has found savings, kept a lid on spending growth and banked revenue upgrades.

'In the upcoming Budget, we'll continue that record of responsible economic management by saving more than we spend and banking all upward revisions to revenue.

'This Budget will be focused on fuel security, addressing inflation, boosting productivity and resilience and managing global economic uncertainty, and today's decision highlights why this is so important.'

# Italy Joins Greece, US, Spain, Germany, Japan, Australia, Barbados and Other Leading Cruise Destinations in Facing Devastating Travel Disruptions as the Middle East Crisis and Hantavirus Outbreak Trigger Massive Cancellations, Route Volatility, Port Closures and Economic Collapse in 2026

Italy joins Greece, the US, Spain, Germany, Japan, Australia, Barbados and other major cruise destinations in facing severe travel disruptions, as escalating tensions in the Middle East and the hantavirus outbreak lead to widespread cruise cancellations, route shifts, port closures and severe economic losses in 2026. The global cruise industry is facing one of its most challenging years in 2026, as the escalating Middle East crisis and the spread of hantavirus on cruise ships create severe disruptions. Key destinations, including Italy, Greece, the US, Spain, Germany, Japan, Australia, and Barbados, are grappling with massive cancellations, route volatility, port closures, and the economic collapse of tourism-dependent sectors. This crisis is reshaping the cruise landscape, threatening local businesses, jobs, and the livelihood of millions. This article dives into the profound effects of these global disruptions on some of the world’s leading cruise destinations.

### **Italy Faces Severe Impact on Cruise Tourism**

Italy’s cruise tourism industry, one of the largest in the Mediterranean, is experiencing an alarming downturn due to geopolitical instability and health concerns. The country’s Civitavecchia, Venice, and Naples ports are seeing significantly fewer cruise ships docking as a result of Middle East route diversions and fears surrounding the hantavirus outbreak. Italy, home to some of the Mediterranean’s most sought-after cruise ports like Civitavecchia (Rome), Venice, and Naples, is experiencing a major disruption. With Italy’s cruise sector contributing billions of euros annually to its economy, this downturn threatens local businesses, tourism jobs, and the livelihood of millions. According to government reports, Italy’s cruise tourism market alone generated €2.5 billion in 2025. However, the recent Middle East crisis has led to the rerouting of ships and a reduced number of passenger arrivals. In addition, the hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship that docked in Italian ports has raised public health concerns, leading to a decline in bookings and heightened scrutiny from health authorities. The Middle East crisis has redirected numerous cruise lines away from the Red Sea and Persian Gulf, forcing them to adjust their itineraries. This, coupled with hantavirus-related fears, has left Italy’s major cruise hubs vulnerable to a significant loss in tourism revenue, directly impacting businesses dependent on the influx of travelers.

### **Greece Struggles with Port Closures and Health Concerns**

Greece is enduring a twin crisis: the ongoing Middle East conflict, which has rerouted cruise vessels, and the hantavirus outbreak, which has prompted stringent health measures. Greek cruise ports, including Piraeus, Santorini, and Mykonos, are witnessing a significant decline in cruise arrivals, severely affecting the local economy. As one of the top Mediterranean cruise destinations, Greece’s ports, including Piraeus, Santorini, and Mykonos, have long been at the heart of the European cruise industry. The Greek economy relies heavily on the cruise sector, which provides jobs and supports industries like hospitality, retail, and transportation. Yet, in 2026, Greece is suffering from both geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and concerns surrounding the hantavirus outbreak. In recent years, Greek ports have seen over 4 million cruise passengers annually, but this number has sharply declined due to ship cancellations and route rerouting. The Middle East crisis has led to avoided routes, diverting ships to other regions. Additionally, hantavirus-related passenger restrictions and quarantine measures have made cruise tourism appear risky to potential travelers, further impacting Greece’s bustling port cities.

### **US Faces Economic Toll with Cruise Cancellations**

The US, a leading source market for cruise tourism, has seen a sharp decline in both inbound and outbound cruise demand. The Middle East crisis has led to itinerary adjustments and a growing number of cancellations. Additionally, the hantavirus scare has added to the challenges, with stricter health measures deterring cruise travelers. The United States, a leading cruise source market, is facing a severe disruption to its cruise operations. With major cruise hubs in Miami, Port Everglades, New Orleans, and Seattle, the US cruise industry contributes more than $53 billion to the national economy annually. According to the US Travel Association, over 15 million Americans embark on cruises every year, making it the world’s largest cruise market. However, the Middle East geopolitical instability has led to massive route changes and cancellations, particularly affecting Caribbean, Mediterranean, and Panama Canal itineraries. In addition, the hantavirus scare, which has caused public health authorities to impose stricter controls on cruise passengers, has led to a significant decline in bookings. Cruise lines, including Carnival and Royal Caribbean, have reported a marked decrease in passenger demand, and port revenues have sharply dropped.

### **Spain’s Ports Suffer Economic Losses from Reduced Cruise Traffic**

Spain, especially Barcelona, one of Europe’s largest cruise ports, is witnessing sharp declines in cruise tourism. The Middle East crisis has reduced port calls in Spanish cities, while the hantavirus outbreak has raised additional concerns over cruise safety, leading to fewer ships docking in Spain. Spain is another major player in the global cruise market, with ports like Barcelona, Palma de Mallorca, and Tenerife attracting millions of cruise passengers each year. In fact, Barcelona is the largest cruise port in Europe, handling more than 3 million cruise passengers annually. The Spanish economy relies heavily on the tourism sector, contributing nearly 14% of Spain’s GDP, with cruise tourism playing a key role in generating revenue for local businesses. In 2026, however, the Middle East crisis has led to altered cruise itineraries, resulting in fewer ships docking in Spain’s ports. This, coupled with the hantavirus outbreak, has prompted additional health checks and travel restrictions for passengers. As a result, Spain’s tourism sector faces losses of millions in potential earnings as cruise passengers seek alternative, safer destinations.

### **Germany Faces Decline in Cruise Tourism Demand**

Germany, a major outbound cruise market, has seen a significant drop in demand for cruises in 2026. With major cruise hubs in Hamburg and Bremerhaven, Germany is feeling the effects of both the Middle East geopolitical tensions and the hantavirus outbreak. Germany, with Hamburg and Bremerhaven as key cruise hubs, is one of the largest outbound markets for cruise travel. In 2025, over 2 million Germans embarked on cruises, with a growing demand for Baltic and Mediterranean cruises. However, in 2026, the geopolitical instability in the Middle East and the hantavirus outbreak are significantly affecting Germany’s cruise market. The country’s health agencies, in collaboration with the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control, have been actively involved in monitoring passengers connected to the hantavirus outbreak aboard a ship in the Mediterranean. These developments have raised safety concerns for Germans planning to travel by sea, and many cruise lines are reporting declines in bookings from German nationals. The result is a sharp downturn in both inbound and outbound tourism, leading to widespread economic repercussions for German ports.

### **Japan’s Cruise Industry Battles Health Concerns and Route Interruptions**

Japan, a growing cruise destination, has been investing heavily in cruise tourism infrastructure. However, the global crisis has caused a sharp decline in cruise arrivals in 2026, with route interruptions and health concerns driving international visitors away. Japan, a growing cruise destination in Asia, has recently invested in expanding its cruise infrastructure, especially in Yokohama, Osaka, and Fukuoka. However, the global disruption caused by the Middle East crisis and the hantavirus outbreak has put the Japanese cruise industry in a precarious position. Although Japan has been increasingly welcoming international cruise travelers, in 2026, the shift in cruise routes, along with the hantavirus scare, has caused an immediate drop in foreign visitor numbers. According to Japan’s Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism, cruise passenger numbers have decreased by nearly 30% in the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same period in the previous year. This loss is impacting local economies, particularly in tourist hotspots, where cruise visitors account for over 5% of the region’s tourism revenue.

Australia, a major player in Asia-Pacific cruise tourism, is facing uncertainty as a result of the Middle East crisis and hantavirus concerns. Australia has seen a surge in cruise tourism in recent years, especially to destinations like the Great Barrier Reef, Sydney, and Tasmania. However, the global crisis in 2026 has caused a steep decline in demand for cruises departing from Australian ports. The Middle East crisis, which has caused disruptions to regional shipping routes, along with the hantavirus concerns, has led to an uptick in cancellations for cruises to Australia. Travelers are avoiding long-haul cruises, particularly those bound for Southeast Asia and the Middle East, fearing the potential exposure to health risks and geopolitical instability. This shift has forced Australian ports to adjust their forecasts, with early projections indicating a 5-10% drop in cruise arrivals in 2026.

Barbados, a leading Caribbean cruise destination, is experiencing a substantial decrease in cruise passengers, particularly after the Middle East crisis and the hantavirus outbreak. Barbados is one of the leading Caribbean cruise destinations, welcoming millions of passengers annually to its shores. The Barbados government relies on cruise tourism for a significant portion of its annual income, particularly in the hospitality and retail sectors. With major cruise lines like Royal Caribbean and Carnival rerouting ships away from the Caribbean to avoid the Middle East conflict zone, Barbados has faced a significant decline in cruise passenger numbers. The global economic fallout from the combined impact of Middle East tensions and the hantavirus outbreak is severe. According to the World Travel & Tourism Council, global tourism revenues have already been significantly impacted, with over $50 billion in cruise tourism spending lost in 2026. The economic impact of the Middle East crisis and hantavirus outbreak on cruise destinations worldwide is severe. Governments and cruise lines are working together to mitigate the damage by adjusting itineraries and increasing health protocols to restore traveler confidence. However, with no clear end in sight to the Middle East crisis and the hantavirus scare, the global cruise industry remains in a fragile state.

news.markdown:
**8:02PM** Sunday, May 10th, 2026

EXPLAINERorange

# Treasurer Jim Chalmers aims to climb out of trouble in Knight toon

Cartoonist Mark Knight plays on reports that Treasurer Jim Chalmers is set to ‘break’ election promises by winding back property investor concessions to help young Aussies buy homes

Mark Knight

5 min read

May 10, 2026 - 2:00PM

Kids News

Print

![Mark Knight's property investment cartoon finds some levity in the investment property tax fears that the federal government is not being honest after leaked tax reform information. Picture: Mark Knight](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/c66f67c564f102d06d75f934109e9d0f)

Mark Knight's property investment cartoon finds some levity in the investment property tax fears that the federal government is not being honest after leaked tax reform information. Picture: Mark Knight

More from Money

[![How to add value to Bluey coins](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/a3da34e3e58a4202675b2ec68121c4ed)](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/money/australia-post-launches-new-2-bluey-coins-after-sellout-success/news-story/fa68685e314df505128a91848b257407)

### [How to add value to Bluey coins](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/money/australia-post-launches-new-2-bluey-coins-after-sellout-success/news-story/fa68685e314df505128a91848b257407)

[![Willy Wonka, eat your heart out](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/5a156d8c7f7fe38af3d4b0b7a67d643b)](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/just-for-fun/australias-own-chocolate-factory-experience-oozes-closer-to-fruition/news-story/8bff1dd23d814be6cd229d45899b5139)

### [Willy Wonka, eat your heart out](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/just-for-fun/australias-own-chocolate-factory-experience-oozes-closer-to-fruition/news-story/8bff1dd23d814be6cd229d45899b5139)

READING LEVEL: ORANGE

It is tough business for young Australians to buy their own home these days. Owning your own patch of Aussie real estate was known as the “Great Australian Dream” once upon a time, but now it seems more of a nightmare. It is even hard to rent a place, with rental properties in the major capital cities becoming scarce and expensive.

That’s why so many young people in their twenties and even thirties continue to live at home with their mum and dad. This is the intergenerational\* wealth debate that we are having as we head into this year’s federal budget\*.

![The “Great Australian Dream” has turned into an unattainable economic nightmare for many Australians. The Albanese Government has pledged changes to help young Australians – but there is a lot of anxiety about what the tax reforms will mean. Picture: composite/Herald Sun Real Estate](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/b5cb6ad5349f31918df2309507864d70)

The “Great Australian Dream” has turned into an unattainable economic nightmare for many Australians. The Albanese Government has pledged changes to help young Australians – but there is a lot of anxiety about what the tax reforms will mean. Picture: composite/Herald Sun Real Estate

Experts believe that homes have become an “investment” market instead of a housing one. They are blaming cashed up Baby Boomers\* supported by government policies like negative gearing\* and the capital gains\* tax (CGT) helping them to raise the price of property and price younger Australians out of the market.

![Real estate prices have meant increasing mortgage stress for first home buyers. Picture: Damian Shaw](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/a6bd0c992ec316eec8a07cdd49a76037)

Real estate prices have meant increasing mortgage stress for first home buyers. Picture: Damian Shaw

These houses are then rented, which provides income for the owners and helps provide a place to live for those who can’t afford to buy a house. We do need to build more homes. More homes mean prices come down. Governments are trying to increase the number of properties constructed annually, particularly as our population grows due to increased immigration\*, but material costs are way up, making building a home way out of the reach of many.

![Housing supply shortages have driven up rental and purchase prices and many young adults simply cannot afford to leave home, a rite of passage that used to begin at 18-23 for most people. Picture: NewsWire/ Damian Shaw](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/92b598e8d561f54d7faf48bcc0d35cc3)

Housing supply shortages have driven up rental and purchase prices and many young adults simply cannot afford to leave home, a rite of passage that used to begin at 18-23 for most people. Picture: NewsWire/ Damian Shaw

So the Federal Government in its pre-budget leaks last week looked like it might try and assist younger property buyers by winding back the tax breaks for property investors. The talk was that they would either heavily reduce negative gearing or overhaul the capital gains tax discount. All in a bid to make it a more level playing field when it comes to buying a house.

![Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and Treasurer Jim Chalmers are trying to calm the panic. Picture: NewsWire / Nikki Short](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/9c35da30a253070976e8440b86f2c2ea)

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and Treasurer Jim Chalmers are trying to calm the panic. Picture: NewsWire / Nikki Short

Now, you political observers may remember, prior to the last election, that Prime Minister Albanese and Treasurer Jim Chalmers said that they would not be touching the CGT and negative gearing. They swore with their hands on their hearts and dismissed journalists’ questions on the matter as a beat-up and pure fallacy\*. It wouldn’t happen, they said, as they stared down the evening news TV cameras. Looks like it will now.

![The Reserve Bank and the government do not always agree the best course of action for the economy. Photo: Canva](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/9136a417a6e23126e71d2db8b6e6513f)

The Reserve Bank and the government do not always agree the best course of action for the economy. Photo: Canva

Politicians’ promises have a use-by date, obviously! Albo won a huge majority at the last election and we are still two years away from the next, so it looks like he’s going to spend some of that political capital on breaking his tax promises. And the federal budget could do with a tax boost, due to the tough economic times. The question many were asking was, is this a fair dinkum\* attempt to reform the housing market or just a tax grab by a government desperate for cash?

![The cost of living crisis and global financial market volatility is producing real stress for Australian families. Picture: file](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/41f7072f132bc75363527828046f7ff1)

The cost of living crisis and global financial market volatility is producing real stress for Australian families. Picture: file

I thought it would be a great subject for a cartoon. Housing policy sounds like a pretty dry topic to cover but I thought I could have a bit of fun with it, particularly because it involved broken promises. With a huge increase in break-and-enters going on in homes across Australia, especially in Melbourne, I thought it might symbolise Treasurer Jim Chalmers taking something that wasn’t his, namely the property tax policies for investors that he said he wouldn’t touch. I pictured him looking a bit sus\* entering a rental property via a front window.

![With consumer confidence down, it is ironically a good time to buy – but who’s got the money? Picture: NewsWire/ Damian Shaw](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/e6fb33d4d6aec668b292e6602d39ddf0)

With consumer confidence down, it is ironically a good time to buy – but who’s got the money? Picture: NewsWire/ Damian Shaw

He’s wearing a black hooded tracksuit so you get the picture. He is carrying a knapsack that he intends, most likely, to fill with the cash windfall\* that the removal of the tax breaks will offer up. To get the Treasurer to explain what he is doing, I thought I would have a police patrol car happen by and shine a torch on Dr Chalmers and ask him what’s going on.

![Australia's Treasurer Jim Chalmers speaks during a television interview during the 2026 IMF and World Bank Group Spring Meetings in Washington, DC, on April 16. Picture: Kent Nishimura/AFP](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/858d742398f865d925a0de6497150a55)

Australia's Treasurer Jim Chalmers speaks during a television interview during the 2026 IMF and World Bank Group Spring Meetings in Washington, DC, on April 16. Picture: Kent Nishimura/AFP

The caption is a play on the word “break” in his reply to the police in the cartoon. The removal of the property investor tax breaks is an attempt to help young home buyers “break” into the housing market, increasing their ability to buy in. On the other hand, we see him “break” into the property investment market in an underhanded\* way, doing something he said he would never do: get rid of the tax advantages for investors.

![With the federal budget scheduled for next week, the Treasurer is not his famously smiley self ahead of announcing the government's fiscal strategy to manage cost-of-living pressures. Picture: NewsWire/Martin Ollman.](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/125918c856b302c4621e6228e2d59131)

With the federal budget scheduled for next week, the Treasurer is not his famously smiley self ahead of announcing the government's fiscal strategy to manage cost-of-living pressures. Picture: NewsWire/Martin Ollman.

Let’s just hope that the Treasurer is right and despite the broken promises, these changes do go some way to making it easier for young Australians to buy their first home. I mean, do you really want to be living with your mum and dad into your thirties?!

**POLL**

**GLOSSARY**

**intergenerational:** involving different generations

**federal budget:** the government’s annual financial report and policy statement to the parliament and the nation, laying out its plan for collecting and spending money

**Baby Boomers:** the generation born during the post-war baby boom, from 1946 up until 1964

**negative gearing:** borrowing money to invest where the return (rental income) from your investment is less than the borrowing cost, for example, housing loan repayments

**capital gains:** the profits that you make when you buy something and then sell it again at a higher price

**immigration:** the act of coming to live permanently in a new country

**fallacy:** falsehood, something that is not true or real

**fair dinkum:** genuine, honest true, a real deal

**underhanded:** done secretly, and sometimes dishonestly, in order to achieve an advantage

sus: suspect, suspicious

**windfall:** a sudden, unexpected piece of good fortune or financial gain

[How war fuels federal budget fires](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/arts/treasurer-takes-shelter-in-budget-bunker-from-economic-missiles/news-story/9d241b5e94627a481f15c288f66f5f6c)

[All aboard RBA runaway rate train](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/money/chalmers-stokes-economic-fire-bullock-hits-brakes-in-knight-toon/news-story/351bbcd48baa0742bde64dcd370c17ce)

[Why Australians are on the move](https://www.kidsnews.com.au/society-culture/aussies-exit-nsw-wa-hits-three-million-population-milestone/news-story/53ff4f59105cf72e51a573c04e6de722)

**QUICK QUIZ**

The government is looking to change tax rules for what type of investment?

What does CGT stand for?

Owning your own home used to be regarded as what?

Who is being priced out of the property market?

What have the older generation known as Baby Boomers got to do with housing prices?

_Curriculum Links: English, Personal and Social, Critical and Creative Thinking_

**VCOP ACTIVITY**

**What happens next?**

Imagine this story is part of an animated series made up of three cartoons. The three cartoons tell the complete story and this article is only Part One. Think about what the rest of the story could be and draw the next two cartoons that tell the story.

# When is the 2026–27 federal budget? Here's what you need to know before tomorrow

- [Topic:Budget](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/budget)

2h ago2 hours agoSun 10 May 2026 at 2:40pm

![A hand holding a $100 note next to a wallet](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/212bd23c8a0b5742a8b73d84df8c3927?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1791&cropW=2692&xPos=188&yPos=197&width=862&height=575)

This year's federal budget is shaping up to be one of the biggest in decades. (ABC News: Claire Moodie)

Federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers will hand down the Albanese government's fifth budget tomorrow night.

And while this will inevitably involve a lot of political talk, it will also, either directly or indirectly, affect your finances.

Here's what to expect and what it could mean for you.

## What is the federal budget?

The federal budget is the government's plan for raising and spending money. It also provides a snapshot of the national economy.

While the comparison is often made to a household budget, it's a poor one: the purpose of a government budget is to help the entire economy, whereas a household budget is mainly concerned with its own financial situation.

Each year, the federal government looks at **how much money it has coming in** (revenue) and **how much money it has going out** (expenditure).

Sources of revenue include personal and business taxes, excises, levies, selling government assets and money generated from government-owned entities.

How revenue is raised and where it is spent can impact your financial situation in one way or another, so it's quite important.

## Why does the budget matter?

The federal budget lays out where the federal government plans to allocate spending over the next few years.

It can either be in surplus, where spending is less than revenue, or deficit, where spending is more than revenue.

This includes things such as our hospitals, schools, roads and the NDIS.

It shapes how our economy functions, so its ripple effects have an impact on all of us, whether we're taxpayers or not.

A good way to get a quick glimpse of these impacts (spending priorities) is by reading what we call our winners and losers list, which sums up which groups will benefit the most from the new plan.

We'll publish this shortly after the budget is released.

## What time is the federal budget speech?

**Tomorrow night.**

Mr Chalmers's speech will be broadcast live from Parliament House in Canberra at:

- **7:30pm AEST:** ACT, New South Wales, Queensland, Victoria and Tasmania
- **7pm ACDT:** Northern Territory and South Australia
- **4:30pm AWST:** Western Australia

It typically **lasts about half an hour.**

![Jim Chalmers and Anthony Albanese sit on a couch holding the budget booklets. They are smiling.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1171aec92b4d8c3a6e7316fa5c29b468?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3256&cropW=4884&xPos=221&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Jim Chalmers (left) will deliver his budget speech at 7:30pm AEST. (ABC News: Adam Kennedy)

Generally speaking, this speech talks up the positive points the government of the day wants to promote and glosses over the negatives.

That's why we have a team of journalists who will be combing through the nitty-gritty details of the budget papers to suss out the real-world impacts of these plans.

## Where can I watch the speech?

It'll be broadcast on free-to-air television on ABC TV as well as on the ABC News Channel.

You can also stream it on [ABC iview](https://iview.abc.net.au/).

And you can [watch the ABC News live stream](https://www.abc.net.au/news/newschannel) via the ABC News website by clicking "watch live" at the top of the page.

We'll also be blogging the whole thing live on the ABC News website.

## Where can I read the budget papers?

On the federal government's [2026–27 budget website](https://budget.gov.au/).

There's only a countdown clock on there at the moment, but it should be updated as soon as Mr Chalmers tables the papers.

## What's in this year's budget?

This year's budget is shaping up to be one of the biggest in decades.

Significant [tax changes are coming](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-05/labor-to-change-cgt-negative-gearing-and-trusts-in-budget/106640096) for property and share investors, electric car drivers and the beneficiaries of more than 818,000 discretionary trusts.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has said the changes are needed to make Australia fairer.

"For many young people, they feel like they haven't got a fair crack compared with my generation and the generations beforehand," Mr Albanese said.

At its core, the budget will contain three central packages: tax reform, savings and productivity and investment.

Here are some items that have been made public prior to budget night.

### Tax reform trio

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-0-tax-reform-trio-content)

The budget will feature [changes to the capital gains tax](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-06/negative-gearing-capital-gains-tax-trusts-explained-simply/106644614) (CGT) discount, negative gearing and the taxation of trust funds.

The CGT discount is expected to be equal to the amount of inflation, but the government may also consider a flat discount such as 25, 30 or 35 per cent.

For negative gearing, Labor could opt to abolish it entirely or to cap the number of properties that can be negatively geared. Several sources told the ABC there would be changes to the way discretionary trusts were taxed, but the specific details remain uncertain.

### EVs

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-1-evs-content)

The government will begin [winding back the tax discount for EVs](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-04/electric-vehicle-tax-discount-to-be-wound-back/106639618) bought under a novated lease, saying electric cars worth more than $75,000 will have to pay some fringe benefits tax from April next year, and all EVs will have to pay from 2029, though at a discounted rate.

### Fuel security package

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-2-fuel-security-package-content)

More than [$10 billion will be spent](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-06/government-commits-10-billion-for-australian-fuel-supply/106582514) bolstering Australia's fuel security as the federal government attempts to shield the nation from the long-term consequences of the war in Iran.

Mr Albanese said $7.5 billion would go towards providing financial support for the supply of fuel and fertiliser, including loans, equity, guarantees and insurance, while $3.2 billion would fund an onshore fuel security reserve.

### Tax deduction for workers

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-3-tax-deduction-for-workers-content)

A [one-off tax cut](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-05/pm-leaves-door-open-for-workers-tax-offset-budget/106642706) of up to $300 this financial year for workers has not been ruled out by Labor. Mr Albanese has refused to confirm whether the offset would be included in the budget.

### NDIS cuts

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-4-ndis-cuts-content)

Health Minister Mark Butler announced [$15 billion would be cut](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-22/mark-butler-ndis-overhaul-eligibility-test/106592186) from the NDIS over the next four years to clamp down on ballooning costs and prevent it from becoming "an ATM for shonks, grifters, fraudsters and crooks".

Initial modelling shows the changes will reduce the number of people on the NDIS from 760,000 to 600,000 by the end of the decade.

### Defence

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-5-defence-content)

An extra [$53 billion will be spent on defence](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-15/defence-spend-53b-next-decade-intensifying-risks-richard-marles/106568890) over the next decade in order to lift military spending to 3 per cent of Australia's GDP.

The spending will be focused on infrastructure such as drones and long-range missiles.

### Receipt-free tax deduction

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-6-receiptfree-tax-deduction-content)

About 6.2 million Australians will be able to claim an instant [$1,000 tax deduction without receipts](https://ministers.treasury.gov.au/ministers/jim-chalmers-2022/media-releases/1000-instant-tax-deduction-deliver-lower-simpler-taxes).

It will come into effect for the 2027 tax season next year.

The minimum tax saving under the legislation will be $470, with the average saving being $205. The legislation will mean workers won't need to provide receipts for up to $1,000 in work-related expenses.

### Private health insurance rebate

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-7-private-health-insurance-rebate-content)

More than [3 million Australians aged over 65](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-22/private-health-for-over-65s-to-cost-hundreds-more/106594174) will have to pay, on average, between $226 and $255 more a year for their private health insurance. Mr Butler said the current policy was "not fair between generations" and not the best use of taxpayer money.

### Countering terrorism

[Read more](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-11/when-is-the-2026-federal-budget/106656890#card-8-countering-terrorism-content)

The federal government will spend $74 million over the next two years to set up a national Counter Terrorism Online Centre in an effort to curb youth radicalisation.

news.title: Chalmers wants to fix inequality. But the latest figures show the wealth gap is growing
news.snippet: The rich really are getting richer, with the gap between Australia's richest households and everyone else widening.
news.description: The rich really are getting richer, with the gap between Australia's richest households and everyone else widening.
news.markdown:
Advertisement [Add us as a preferred source on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=smh.com.au) Listen to this article 4 min Advertisement A key indicator of household wealth has stagnated over the past five years as the gap between Australia’s richest households and everyone else widens. Median household wealth – the middle value for all households - has been stuck around $700,000 since 2019-20 despite a surge the value of housing and other assets since the COVID-19 pandemic. ![Median household wealth in Australia stalled during the past five years despite rising house prices.](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.228%2C$multiply_0.7725%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_73%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/q_86%2Cf_auto/77ef768d9ece5d33a4fc0f35faa5732767e0c597) Median household wealth in Australia stalled during the past five years despite rising house prices.Wolter Peeters New modelling by KPMG shows the post-pandemic asset boom, fuelled by ultra-low interest rates, disproportionately benefited the richest fifth of households, which have net wealth of $1.6 million and above. That group, which has grown from 15 per cent to 22 per cent of households over the past decade, has benefited from strong gains in property and share markets, along with rapid debt reduction. “For these households, wealth hasn’t just grown, it’s multiplied into the millions,” the report says. About a third of Australian households – a total of 3.35 million – hold less than $300,000 in wealth, which is less than half the nationwide median wealth and just 19 per cent of [Australia’s average household wealth.](https://www.smh.com.au/business/the-economy/boomers-are-reshaping-the-way-wealth-is-distributed-these-charts-show-how-20260130-p5nycb.html) How household wealth is distributed in Australia ### How household wealth is distributed in Australia 2024-25 dollars, adjusted for inflation | | | | | --- | --- | --- | | Less than $300,000 | 2019-20 | 2024-25 | | Number of households | 3.23 million | 3.35 million | | Share of all households | 33.2% | 31% | | $300,000 - $900,000 | 2019-20 | 2024-25 | | Number of households | 3.06 million | 3 million | | Share of all households | 31.4% | 27.7% | | $900,000 - $1.6m | 2019-20 | 2024-25 | | Number of households | 1.75 million | 2.07 million | | Share of all households | 18% | 19.2% | | $1.6million + | 2019-20 | 2024-25 | | Number of households | 1.69 million | 2.38 million | | Share of all households | 17.4% | 22% | Table with 3 columns and 12 rows. (column headers with buttons are sortable) The median is the middle value for all households Source:KPMG, ABS This group includes pensioners and other low-income groups who don’t own a home, along with younger people yet to accumulate significant assets. KPMG urban economist Terry Rawnsley, who conducted the analysis, said this low wealth segment of the population had missed out on the post-pandemic asset boom. Advertisement Advertisement “Without housing or investment assets, wealth for these households has been largely stagnant,” he said. The wealth findings come as the federal government prepares to unveil measures to promote “intergenerational” equity in Tuesday’s federal budget. Treasurer Jim Chalmers is expected to scale back provisions that favour property investors, [i](https://www.smh.com.au/national/capital-gains-tax-and-negative-gearing-reforms-are-overdue-20260508-p5zv1b.html) [ncluding the 50 per cent capital gains discount and negative gearing rules](https://www.smh.com.au/national/capital-gains-tax-and-negative-gearing-reforms-are-overdue-20260508-p5zv1b.html). An increase to the minimum tax on discretionary trust distributions is also anticipated. “I think it’s really clear to a lot of Australians that the housing market and the tax system is making it harder for people, particularly for younger people,” Chalmers said last week. Family budgets have been sapped by a lingering post-pandemic cost-of-living crunch stoked by elevated inflation and higher interest rates. KPMG’s analysis shows those factors have coincided with stagnating wealth for millions of households. “These numbers help explain the frustration we’re seeing as people feel they’re not getting ahead but see the top end of town pulling away,” said Rawnsley. The difference between the average wealth and median wealth of households during the past five years highlights the growing wealth disparity. Average household wealth surged from $1.26 million to $1.56 million between 2019-20 and 2024-25, an increase of 24 per cent in real terms. Average and median household wealth in Australia ### Average and median household wealth in Australia 2024-25 dollars, adjusted for inflation | | Median Value | Average value | | --- | --- | --- | | 2009-10 | $629,000
$629,000
$629,000 | $1,064,000
$1,064,000
$1,064,000 | | 2014-15 | $633,000
$633,000
$633,000 | $1,142,000
$1,142,000
$1,142,000 | | 2019-20 | $701,000
$701,000
$701,000 | $1,262,000
$1,262,000
$1,262,000 | | 2024-25 | $700,000
$700,000
$700,000 | $1,560,000
$1,560,000
$1,560,000 | Table with 3 columns and 4 rows. (column headers with buttons are sortable) The median is the middle value for all households Source:KPMG, ABS But the median, or middle value, of household wealth was basically unchanged at about $700,000 during that period. Only about one-fifth of households have the nation’s average wealth level, or higher, the KPMG modelling shows. “The data suggests wealth growth has been concentrated among the richest households, rather than reflecting broad improvements across the community,” said Rawnsley. “Asset owners surged ahead, while households without property or investments were largely left standing still, despite superannuation helping the poorest households.” The number of households within the middle band of net wealth, between $300,000 and $900,000, has declined during the past decade, because rising house prices have lifted some in that group into the higher $900,000 to $1.6 million bracket, which has grown as a share of the population during the past five years. Households in the $900,000 to $1.6 million bracket had enough equity in their homes to benefit from the post-pandemic asset surge. “The compounding effect of property and superannuation really shows up in this group and, with mortgage debt largely under control, wealth accumulation continues to accelerate very quickly,” Rawnsley said. _**Get the day’s breaking news, entertainment ideas and a long read to enjoy. # The 'alarming' inflation rise to 4.6 per cent is the new normal for Aussies living under the high debt, big spending Albanese govt

The Reserve Bank’s decision to raise the cash rate to 4.35 per cent off the back of Iran war-related price shocks lays bare the self-inflicted costs of our national indifference to fuel sovereignty, writes Saxon Davidson.


Saxon DavidsonInstitute of Public Affairs Research Fellow

3 min read

May 11, 2026 - 5:00AM

[Sky News Australia](https://www.skynews.com.au/)

![](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/118a4a4f4c224b73b684acec682e8c1a)

Treasurer Jim Chalmers says the Labor government’s budget has been difficult to finalise due to unpredictability in the global economy.

“This one’s been especially difficult to finalise because of these international developments that you’ve been covering,” Mr Chalmers told Sky News Australia.

“Every day there’s been some unpredictability in the global economy, and that’s meant we’ve taken the time to get it right.”

The Reserve Bank’s decision on Tuesday to raise the cash rate to 4.35 per cent off the back of the Iran war-related price shocks lays bare the self-inflicted costs of Australia being a fuel importer.

While elevated levels of inflation have become normalised by the high debt, big spending Albanese government, the RBA’s most recent decision attributes the cash rate rise to “developments in the Middle East”.

These are not only adding to inflation but “likely to have second-round effects on prices for goods and services more broadly”.

The consumer price index, released monthly by the Australian Bureau of Statistics, revealed last week that annual inflation in March 2026 was 4.6 per cent, up from 3.7 per cent the previous month.

The alarming increase was off the back of an increase in the transport price category, with automotive fuel prices surging nearly 33 per cent from February to March alone.

![](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/322c1b1b905cf8b70a46737b15440796)

Sky News host Rowan Dean responds to President Donald Trump's criticisms of Australia over its ties to China.

“Labor Party loves China,” Mr Dean told Sky News host Steve Price.

“It actually terrifies me when we have Penny Wong wandering around saying I’m doing energy deals with China.

“At the drop of a hat, they will subvert our economy or our trade with them.

“This is the same mob who were wandering around our oceans firing their weapons willy nilly.

“The Albanese government is a joke when it comes to China, and it should concern all Australians.”

That the dramatic rise in transport CPI has been caused by the conflict in Iran is not in doubt.

What is also not in doubt is how Australia decided to voluntarily make itself vulnerable to these shocks in the first place.

While the Prime Minister did not cause the war, successive governments have exposed Australia to those shocks by accepting that Australia should become dependent on those global supply chains, despite our abundant capacity to be self-reliant .

We only have two oil refineries left in Australia and have become overwhelmingly reliant on imported fuel.

Loading embed...

In 2002 we imported only 11 per cent of our refined petroleum, we now import 90 per cent of it, and we are one the largest importer of diesel in the world.

Not only are we not self sufficient in refining our own fuel, we only have a month of reserve fuel, meaning that our fuel safety net is essentially non-existent.

The Prime Minister has made deals with neighbouring countries to provide with an immediate short-term supply of fuel, however this is not a sustainable solution.

Decades of poor decision-making has led us to a place of vulnerability, however, the government’s policy of net zero emissions is exacerbating this crisis by committing the government to forcing our most emission intensive industries to shut down – the same industries that would make self-reliance possible.

Reserve Bank of Australia Governor, Michele Bullock appears at a public hearing, reviewing the Reserve bank of Australia Annual report 2025 Parliament House in Canberra. Picture: NewsWire / Martin Ollman

The safeguard mechanism is the federal government’s marquee net zero policy that mandates large projects and facilities to reduce their emissions or purchase carbon credits to continue to operate – a form of carbon tax on Australian industry.

IPA research found that the total carbon tax placed on these projects and facilities will be between $7.1 billion and $11.7 billion by 2029-2030.

For Australia’s last two refineries, the safeguard mechanism will impose a combined $165.5 million carbon tax.

The obvious consequence of prioritising emission reductions throughout the economy is that we will become dependent on importing from countries who have different priorities.

This is short-sighted considering we are a nation that relies on our truckies to transport goods to our regions, and from our regions to our capital cities, and therefore the rise in cost to transport those goods impacts the price of everything that is transported, especially food.

We as a nation need a plan that prioritises our sovereign capability and improves our fuel security.

The opposition recently announced a plan to double our reserve fuel from 30 to 60 days, and earlier this year dropped their commitment to the policy of net zero and want to repeal the safeguard mechanism.

This announcement was followed a week later by the Albanese government announcing a $10 billion plan to ensure at least 50 days of fuel supply and storage of diesel and aviation fuel and establish a permanent fuel security reserve in Australia.

This represents a positive shift in policy direction from both major parties, especially given they both own the historical blame for our fuel woes.

We must use the lessons of the current fuel crisis to ensure Australia can survive as an independent and self-reliant nation, because the status quo of the last 25 years has not worked, and we are now paying the price.

_Saxon Davidson is a Research Fellow at the Institute of Public Affairs_
